data.title: Comparative Study of Compensation Performance for Disturbance Observers Implemented Using Artificial Neural Network and Spiking Neural Network in Closed-Loop System

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1149/ma2025-01633078mtgabs?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1149/ma2025-01633078mtgabs, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract:
In this study, compensation performances of two Disturbance Observers (DOBs) implemented using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and a Spiking Neural Network (SNN) are compared through simulations to confirm feasibilities of adopting SNN structures to DOBs for closed-loop control systems. In most automatic control applications, a control loop is designed to make a target system follow given reference commands in real-time. A basic form of the loop contains a difference operator which generates errors between the reference commands and actual responses, and a controller which calculates control inputs to minimize these errors. However, control performance of the basic loop system can be degraded when unexpected changes of system characteristics or external disturbances occur during operation. In order to mitigate this degradation, DOBs are typically added to the loop system. Using a mathematical system model which produces ideal control responses with the above loop system, DOBs calculate additional compensation inputs to minimize the differences between actual and ideal responses.
 Among various DOBs, ANN-based DOBs (ANN-DOBs) have demonstrated superior performance in numerous control applications. In ANN-DOBs, compensation inputs are generated through inference operations of an ANN structure using obtainable system state variables as network inputs. Network weights are updated with conventional learning processes based on error-related cost function minimization. However, significant computational burdens are required to operate ANN-DOBs in real-time systems, because the learning and inference processes must be completed within each control cycle period. Switching the network structure from ANN to SNN is a potential solution to the computation issues. In the SNN, learning and inference operations occur through spike signal flows and interactions without any computations.
 To evaluate feasibilities of adopting SNN structures to DOB in control systems, compensation performance comparison tests between ANN-DOB and SNN-DOB are conducted via simulations under identical system characteristic change scenarios. A Radial Basis Function-based Neural Network (RBFNN) [1] with backpropagation learning is used to implement the ANN-DOB, and a Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) model based SNN with Reward-modulated Spike Timing-Dependent Plasticity (R-STDP) [2] learning is used to implement the SNN-DOB. For both networks, same inputs and same number of layers and nodes are used. Step response simulation results show that for both DOBs, rise and fall times are similarly improved compared to the times obtained without any DOB. For steady-state oscillations, amounts of oscillations are reduced by a factor of 0.01 for the ANN-DOB and 0.1 for the SNN-DOB, compared to the amount obtained without a DOB.
 [1] Liu, J. “Radial Basis Function (RBF) neural network control for mechanical systems: design, analysis and Matlab simulation.” Springer Science & Business Media (2013).
 [2] Seung, H. S. "Learning in spiking neural networks by reinforcement of stochastic synaptic transmission." Neuron 40.6 (2003): 1063-1073.

data.title: Neural learning for the emergence of social norms in multiagent systems

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/AGENTS.2017.8015298?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/AGENTS.2017.8015298, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Social norms such as social rules and conventions play a pivotal role in sustaining system order by facilitating coordination and cooperation in multiagent systems. This paper studies the neural basis for the emergence of social norms in multiagent systems by modeling each agent as a spiking neural system with a learning capability through reinforcement of stochastic synaptic transmission. A spiking neural learning model is proposed to encode the interaction information in the input spike train of the neural network, and decode the agents' decisions in the output spike train. Learning takes place in the synapses in terms of changing its firing rate, based on the presynaptic spike train, an eligibility trace that records the synaptic actions and the reinforcement feedback from the interactions. Experimental results show that this basic neural level of learning is capable of maintaining emergence of social norms and different learning parameters and encoding methods in the neural system can bring about various macro emergence phenomenon. This paper makes an initial step towards understanding the correlation between neural synaptic activities and global social consistency, and revealing neural mechanisms underlying agent behavioral level of decision making in multiagent systems.
